message.content: Adversaries may execute a Network Denial of Service (DoS) attack by directly flooding a target with high volumes of network traffic, aiming to disrupt the availability and functionality of the targeted systems and networks. This technique, known as Direct Network Flood, can utilize various network protocols, including stateless ones like UDP or ICMP, and stateful ones like TCP. Botnets are frequently employed to generate the necessary traffic, leveraging numerous compromised systems globally to overwhelm the target. Adversaries might either build their own botnet infrastructure or rent existing ones. The distributed nature of these attacks (DDoS) makes it challenging to differentiate malicious traffic from legitimate users, especially when the attack is widespread. High-profile incidents, such as the 2012 attacks on major US banks, exemplify the severe impact of such tactics. Mitigation strategies include implementing robust network traffic monitoring, rate limiting, and employing anti-DDoS services to detect and mitigate abnormal traffic patterns. Additionally, maintaining an incident response plan and collaborating with ISPs can help in managing and mitigating the effects of such attacks.
